food_name: Sambal Bos Marah (Angry Boss Chilli Sambal)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indonesian sambal; FOODON_03301710 (chili pepper sauce) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (chilli sauces and pastes) used as closest parent; no specific sambal code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for sambal or Indonesian chilli condiment at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical condiment serving of sambal is approximately 20 g (about one heaped tablespoon), used as a side dip or topping alongside a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chilli paste with oil and garlic) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Hot sauce, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 170931) and Chili oil (FDC ID 171413), combined estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Indonesian sambal of this formulation. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for chilli hot sauce (FDC 170931) and chilli oil (FDC 171413), weighted to reflect a sambal composition of approximately 50% crushed chilli, 35% vegetable oil, 10% garlic, and 5% salt. Energy, fat, and sodium are the dominant nutritional drivers. FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for chilli-based condiments were also consulted as a cross-reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chilli-based condiments and hot sauces — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chilli sauce and vegetable-oil-based condiments
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indonesian sambal. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chilli sauce and similar oil-dominant, low-carbohydrate condiments. The low carbohydrate content (12 g/100 g) and high fat and fibre content strongly suppress glycemic response. Glycemic load per 20 g serving = (15 × 12 × 0.20) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sambal intake to small condiment portions (≤10 g per serve) due to its elevated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g, which can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets when consumed in larger quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~15) and low glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of under 0.5 per standard 20 g serving, making it a low-glycemic condiment that is unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation when used in typical condiment quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile with beneficial capsaicin content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat in this sambal is predominantly unsaturated (monounsaturated and polyunsaturated), with low saturated fat (approximately 2.5 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol; capsaicin from chillies has been associated with anti-inflammatory and vasodilatory effects in peer-reviewed literature, though sodium intake should still be moderated in cardiovascular patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Bos Marah (Angry Boss Chilli Sambal) is an Indonesian chilli-based condiment made from crushed fresh red chillies, garlic, and vegetable oil, blended into a coarse, intensely spicy paste. The name translates loosely as 'Angry Boss Sambal', reflecting its aggressive heat level (rated Level 8 on a spice scale). The texture is semi-chunky and oily, with a vivid red colour from the high chilli content. The flavour profile is sharp, fiery, and pungent, with garlic providing aromatic depth and oil lending a smooth, coating mouthfeel. Sambal is a foundational condiment in Indonesian and broader Southeast Asian cuisine, traditionally served alongside rice dishes, martabak (stuffed pancakes), grilled meats, and fried foods. Nutritionally, this sambal is calorie-moderate at approximately 220 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by its oil content (18 g fat/100 g). The fat profile is predominantly unsaturated, which is favourable for cardiovascular health. Carbohydrate content is low (12 g/100 g), yielding a low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 480 mg/100 g, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension when consuming larger portions. The chilli base provides meaningful vitamin C (approximately 45 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor). Capsaicin, the active compound in chillies, has documented anti-inflammatory and thermogenic properties in peer-reviewed literature. This product is vegan, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. It is served as a condiment in small portions (typically 10–20 g) alongside main dishes.
common_user_queries: What are the ingredients in angry boss chilli?
What is the glycemic index of sambal?

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific branded sambal product exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for chilli hot sauce and chilli oil, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar oil-dominant, low-carbohydrate chilli condiments. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (FDC 170931 hot sauce; FDC 171413 chilli oil) weighted to reflect the described sambal composition (approximately 50% crushed chilli, 35% vegetable oil, 10% garlic, 5% salt). No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific product. 2. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries used for this estimation. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference USDA entries. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based condiment containing no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based condiment with no UV-exposed mushrooms or animal ingredients; vitamin D is naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated oils are present in the described formulation; trans fat is naturally zero. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): entirely plant-based formulation; cholesterol is naturally zero. 4. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for chilli sauce and oil-dominant condiments. Glycemic load per 20 g serving calculated as (15 × 12 × 0.20) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4. 5. This is a branded restaurant product from Smile Martabak - Funan (field survey data). The food_name has been normalised to a canonical English form. The promotional tagline from the input description has been excluded from the canonical record. 6. Indonesian cuisine requires Indonesian local name only (per multilingual rules); the Indonesian name 'Sambal Bos Marah' is the native product name and satisfies this requirement. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for Indonesian sambal at this specificity.
